explicit_text: I was born in Fukushima Prefecture in 1899. My early childhood was spent there under the care of my grandparents since my father left for America when I was just five months old. Although my mother joined my father later, my roots and early memories are deeply tied to Fukushima.

explicit_text: I went to Tokyo to attend the Iwakuni Railroad School, hoping to advance my education. However, since the school wasn’t recognized by the Ministry of Education, I couldn’t proceed to higher education there and had to attend a high school instead. My life in Tokyo shaped my early academic experiences before I eventually came to America.

explicit_text: I boarded the ship to America from Yokohama after passing strict health examinations, including for Trachoma, which I had failed twice before finally passing. When I returned to Japan to find a bride, I saw Yokohama filled with young couples, which was a memorable sight. Yokohama was a key port in my journey between Japan and America.

explicit_text: I arrived in San Francisco aboard the Nihon Maru after a stop in Hawaii. When I first met my father there, I recognized him from his photograph, but I wasn’t impressed by the city since I was used to Tokyo’s Marunouchi district. San Francisco was my first American city and the place where I reunited with my father.

explicit_text: I came to America on the ship Nihon Maru, traveling from Yokohama to San Francisco with a stop in Hawaii. Onboard, I remember buying bananas in Hawaii and drinking soda water for the first time. The journey was significant as it marked my transition from Japan to America.

explicit_text: I came to Madera in 1920 where my father and his partner bought 100 acres of land to farm. This was the place where we established our family farm, growing grapes and other crops. Madera was also where community organizations like Shinwa Kai and the Japanese Language School were founded, and where I became involved in local Japanese associations.

explicit_text: When World War I broke out, my father made a profit from farming grapes and peaches despite the challenges. The war period was a time of brisk business, and it helped our family establish a foothold in California agriculture. However, it was also a time when anti-Japanese land laws were enacted, complicating our efforts to buy land.

explicit_text: When I first came to America, I went to Selma where my father was leasing land. However, the farming results there were not good, so we moved to Parlier soon after. Selma was the initial place where my family tried to establish farming in America.

explicit_text: After Selma, we moved to Parlier where my father farmed 80 acres of grapes and peaches. Parlier was a more successful farming location for us, and it was part of our early efforts to build a stable livelihood in California. explicit_text: The Great Depression hit us hard, especially with the price of grapes dropping drastically to 35 dollars a ton at the lowest point. Many people sold their land and left, but my family held on, encouraged by my father's determination to not give up what he had built. We diversified by growing vegetables and worked tirelessly to survive through those tough years.

explicit_text: One of my brothers attended the University of California, Berkeley while we struggled to maintain our farm during the Depression. His education was a source of hope and support for our family. Berkeley represented an important opportunity for our younger generation.

explicit_text: In 1921, Shinwa Kai was organized in Madera, and I became its president before the war. We celebrated New Year's in a storage room, showing how close-knit and resourceful our community was. Shinwa Kai was an important organization for Japanese immigrants like me to maintain cultural ties and mutual support.

explicit_text: The Japanese Language School was founded in 1923 in Madera, and I served as its principal before the war. explicit_text: I was recommended as one of three directors of the Fresno Japanese Association when I was 26 and served until the outbreak of the war. My duties included handling consulate work like death and marriage reports, mediating disputes, and lobbying against anti-Japanese legislation. The association was central to our community's welfare and political efforts.

explicit_text: When the anti-Japanese Land Act was proposed in the California State Assembly, our community had to raise money to lobby against it. I was involved in organizing funds and hiring secretaries to fight the bill. The Assembly's actions directly threatened our ability to own land and sustain our farms.

explicit_text: Although I did not speak much about the attack on Pearl Harbor itself, it was the event that led to the war breaking out and changed everything for Japanese Americans like me. The war disrupted our lives and eventually led to the forced removal and incarceration of our community. Pearl Harbor was a turning point in our history in America.
